cit.q: kaḻal malku pantoṭu ammāṉai muṟṟil kaṟṟavar ciṟṟu iṭaik kaṉṉimārkaḷ
young unmarried girls who have a small waist and have learnt the games of playing with molucca beans, large number of balls, ammāṉai, and small winnowsTranslated by .
kaḻal kaṉi vakutta tuṇaic cil lōtik | kuṟum toṭi makaḷir

sense.note: Here the p.u. for line 23 with an unusually detailed description of the women’s hair-do reads: kaḻaṉmaṇiyiṉatu kaṉiyāl kūṟupaṭuttuc curuṭṭappaṭṭa iṉamākiya cilavākiya paṉiccai, presumably “a Paṉiccai-mode in which there are few that are groups [of hair] which have been rolled in portions by means of the ripe fruit of the Kaḻaṉmaṇi”. Here one would expect Kaḻaṉmaṇi to be a proper name for a plant, but this is not attested or discussed elsewhere. Instead, for kaḻal a sub-entry with reference to this passage is found in the TL, which equates kaḻal here with kaḻaṟci, “molucca bean”. This does not appear justified either by the text of the PN nor by its commentary. Of course we may speculate that women used the molucca pods as curlers, but one would want parallels. Perhaps another trace of the general idea is found in Tēvāram 1.4.2.1 where kaḻal is enumerated under a series of girl’s games and translated by VMS as moluca beans (a game otherwise known under the name of kaḻaṅku.

bibl.title: A comprehensive Tamil and English dictionary (Winslow)
